title: GUIDELINES FOR DEVELOPING HARMONIZED FOOD SAFETY LEGISLATION

sections.section: GUIDELINES FOR DEVELOPING HARMONIZED FOOD SAFETY LEGISLATION
sections.summary: Document title: ‘Guidelines for Developing Harmonized Food Safety Legislation’.
Identifies the document as applicable to the CCAFRICA region with code CXG 98-2022.
Indicates the guidelines were adopted in 2022.

Explains that food safety is critical to public health, food security and trade, notes challenges in CCAFRICA Member Countries (fragmented legislation, low investment and enforcement lapses) that impede trade and consumer protection, and stresses the need to harmonize legislation to align with regional and international standards; the guidelines are developed to guide Member Countries to strengthen food safety legislation and control systems, aligned with Codex, support trade (including AfCFTA), define stakeholder roles, and encourage investment in verification capacity.
Scope: provides guidance to CCAFRICA Member Countries for developing or updating food safety legislation across the entire food chain for food traded nationally, regionally and internationally.

Purpose: to enable CCAFRICA Member Countries to develop harmonized, science- and risk-based food safety legislation, to provide general concepts and principles for coordinated national and regional food control systems, and to promote an integrated food-chain approach that facilitates intra-African and international trade.

Provides definitions to establish common understanding of terms used in the guidelines, including competent authority, consumer, food, food business/operator, food chain, food control and food control system, official control and requirements, and risk-related terms (risk, risk analysis, risk assessment, risk communication and risk management), with cross-references to Codex texts.

sections.section: 5. PRINCIPLES OF A FOOD SAFETY LEGISLATION
sections.summary: Guidance for developing/updating national food safety legislation: use open stakeholder consultation where appropriate; allow provisional sanitary/phytosanitary measures when scientific evidence is insufficient while seeking data; and base laws on eight principles—consumer health protection; a farm-to-fork food chain approach; clearly defined roles/responsibilities for governments, FBOs, consumers and academia; science- and risk-based drafting; cooperation/coordination; equivalence/mutual recognition to facilitate trade; embedding legislation in a national policy framework with regular review; and harmonization with Codex and WTO texts (Codex as fallback where national rules are absent).

Food safety legislation should provide a mechanism for transparent, effective communication and coordination among all institutions responsible for food safety along the food chain and include provisions supporting food safety education, communication and training.
Food safety legislation should include the principle of equivalence and, where applicable, mutual recognition to facilitate trade among CCAFRICA Member Countries.
Food safety legislation should form part of a robust national policy framework and include mechanisms for regular review and updating.

When designing national food safety laws CCAFRICA Member Countries should aim for harmonization to facilitate intra‑African and international trade and protect consumer health, consider relevant Codex Alimentarius and WTO principles when drafting or updating legislation, and use Codex texts where specific national regulations are absent.

Outlines required elements of food safety legislation consistent with national drafting practice and focused on protecting consumer health and fair trade, including an appropriate title, preamble, clear definitions, stated objectives (including risk analysis), guiding principles, enabling and administrative provisions, substantive measures (e.g. licensing, inspections, hygiene, labelling, traceability, recalls, import/export controls and fees), enforcement, offences/fines, regulation-making powers, and transitional/miscellaneous provisions for implementation.

Legislation should set basic guiding principles—drawing on Section 5—including integrity, impartiality, accountability, transparency, fairness and management of conflicts of interest, particularly in official control implementation.

Enabling provisions should define the nature and limits of powers under the law and designate competent authorities empowered to make implementing rules and intervene to ensure compliance with the legislation.
Administrative provisions should establish the institutional structures needed to enforce the law, such as single or multiple food safety agencies, integrated agencies, and coordination authorities.

Substantive provisions must address food safety across the entire food chain and include risk‑based preventive approaches and controls such as licensing, risk analysis, inspection, certification, monitoring and surveillance, hygiene standards, anti‑fraud measures, FBO responsibilities, labelling, traceability, recalls, import/export controls and mechanisms (e.g. fees) to support official controls.
Enforcement provisions should enable measures to ensure compliance, including administrative actions, sanctions, seizure, inspection, prohibitions and prosecution by enforcement agencies.
The legislation should specify the offences covered and provide for fines and penalties, either by direct listing or by reference.
The law should include powers to develop regulations and other measures to support implementation, guided by the Act's objectives.

Miscellaneous provisions should provide for efficient implementation when the law comes into effect, including repeal, savings, transitional arrangements, entry into force and consequential amendments as appropriate to national practice.
